COUNCIL MEETING MINUTES  
 
APRIL 14, 2026  
 
A Meeting of the Council  of the City of Vancouver  was held on Tuesday, April 14, 2026 , at 
9:31 am, in the Council Chamber, Third Floor, City Hall.  This Council meeting was convened  in 
person and  via electronic means as authorized by Part 14 of the Procedure By -law. 
 
PRESENT:  Mayor Ken Sim  
Councillor Rebecca Bligh * (Leave of Absence – Medical  Leave 
from 1 1 am onwards ) 
Councillor Lisa Dominato  
Councillor Pete Fry  
Councillor Sarah Kirby -Yung * (Leave of Absence – Personal 
Reasons ) 
Councillor Mike Klassen  
Councillor Lucy Maloney  
Councillor Peter Meiszner  
Councillor Brian Montague  
Councillor Sean Orr  
Councillor Lenny Zhou  
 
CITY MANAGER’S OFFICE:  Donny van Dyk, City Manager  
Sandra Singh, Deputy City Manager  
 
CITY CLERK’S OFFICE:  Katrina Leckovic, City Clerk  
Bonnie Kennett , Meeting Coordinator  
 
* Denotes absence for a portion of the meeting . 
 
 
WELCOME  
 
The Mayor  acknowledged we are on the unceded homelands of the Musqueam, Squamish, and 
Tsleil -Waututh People. We thank them for having cared for this land and look forward to working 
with them in partnership as we continue to build this great city together . 
 
The Mayor  also recognized the immense contributions of the City of Vancouver’s team 
members who work hard every day to help make our city an incredible place to live, work, and 
play. 
 
 
   

 
Council Meeting  
Minutes, April 14, 2026  2 
 
 
IN CAMERA MEETING  
 
MOVED by Councillor Dominato  
SECONDED by Councillor Klassen  
 
THAT Council will go into meetings later this week which are closed to the public, 
pursuant to Section 165.2(1) of the Vancouver Charter , to discuss matters related to 
paragraphs:  
 
(a)  personal information about an identifiable individual who holds or is being 
considered for a position as an officer, employee or agent of the city or another 
position appointed by the city;  
 
(c)  labour relations or other employee relations;  
 
(g)  litigation or potential litigation affecting the city;  
 
(i)  the receipt of advice that is subject to solicitor -client privilege, including 
communications necessary for that purpose;  
 
(k)  negotiations and related discussions respecting the proposed provision of an 
activity, work or facility that are at their preliminary stages and that, in the view of 
the Council, could reasonably be expected to harm the interests of the city if they 
were h eld in public;  
 
FURTHER THAT Council will go into meetings later this week which are closed to the 
public, pursuant to Section 165.2(2) of the Vancouver Charter , to discuss matters related 
to paragraph:  
 
(b)  the consideration of information received and held in confidence relating to 
negotiations  
 
(i) between the city and a provincial government or the federal government 
or both, or between a provincial government or the federal government or 
both and a third party.  
 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY  
(Councillor Kirby -Yung absent for the vote)

REPORTS  
 
1. 2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver  
April 1, 2026  
 
The General Manager , Arts, Culture and Community Services along with staff from Arts, Culture 
and Community Services, provided a presentation and responded to questions.  
 
MOVED by Councillor Dominato  
SECONDED by Councillor  Klassen  
 
A. THAT Council receive the Report dated April 1, 2026, entitled “2025 Homeless 
Count – Vancouver”  for information.  
 
B. THAT Council call on the Provinc ial and Federal Governments to:   
 
a. Increase investments in income  assistance, disability and rent supports, 
and resources to address intimate partner violence contributing to 
homelessness;  
 
b. Re-commit to and  establish a homeless navigation centre;   
  
c. Expand access to mental health and substance use treatment and 
recovery services  across the Metro Vancouver region and throughout 
British Columbia, including in suburban and smaller municipalities, to 
ensure a more balanced geographic distribution of servi ces and access 
close to home and community;   
  
d. Strengthen discharge planning requirements across health care, 
corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent individuals from being 
discharged into homelessness; and   
  
e. Provide support  to Local Governments for daytime sheltering.   
 
amended  
 
  
 
Council Meeting  
Minutes, April 14, 2026  4 
 
 
AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Fry  
SECONDED by Councillor Maloney  
 
THAT the words “and housing with supports” be added to the end of e.  
 
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11512)  
(Councillor Kirby -Yung absent for the vote)  
 
The amendment having carried, the motion as amended was put and CARRIED 
UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11513)  with Councillor Kirby -Yung absent for the  vote.  
 
FINAL MOTION AS APPROVED  
 
A. THAT Council receive the Report dated April 1, 2026, entitled “2025 Homeless 
Count – Vancouver” for information.  
 
B. THAT Council call on the Provinc ial and Federal Governments to:   
 
a. Increase investments in income  assistance, disability and rent supports, 
and resources to address intimate partner violence contributing to 
homelessness;  
 
b. Re-commit to and establish a homeless navigation centre;   
  
c. Expand access to mental health and substance use treatment and 
recovery services  across the Metro Vancouver region and throughout 
British Columbia, including in suburban and smaller municipalities, to 
ensure a more balanced geographic distribution of services and access 
close to home and community;   
  
d. Strengthen discharge planning requirements across health care, 
corrections, and child welfare systems to prevent individuals from being 
discharged into homelessness; and   
  
e. Provide support  to Local Governments for daytime sheltering  and housing 
with supports.

6. Coastal Flood Management Policy  
March 17, 2026  
 
Staff from Planning, Urban Design and Sustainability and Legal Services responded to 
questions.  
 
MOVED by Councillor Maloney  
SECONDED by Councillor Meiszner  
 
THAT Council a dopt the Coastal Flood Management Policy, generally as presented in 
Appendix A  of the R eport dated March 17, 2026, entitled “ Coastal Flood Management 
Policy ,” which articulates  the City’s role and support s planning , implementation , and 
management  of structural and non -structural coastal flood management  measures  in 
Vancouver.  
 
carried  
 
  
 
Council Meeting  
Minutes, April 14, 2026  10 
 
 
AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Maloney  
SECONDED by Councillor Orr 
 
A. THAT Council adopt the Coastal Flood Management Policy, generally as 
presented in Appendix A to the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Coastal 
Flood Management Policy.”  
 
B.  THAT Council directs staff to report back on the scope of work, governance 
options, resource requirements, and timeline necessary for the City to take the 
steps necessary to convene, or cause to be convened, an intergovernmental and 
inter-agency task force  for the False Creek floodplain, for the purpose of:  
 
1. identifying major publicly owned, utility, transportation, health, and civic 
assets at risk from coastal flooding and sea level rise;  
 
2. assessing whether a coordinated, area -wide structural flood management 
approach would be more effective and cost -efficient than fragmented site -
by-site measures;  
 
3. identifying the appropriate roles of the City, Province, Government of 
Canada, First Nations, agencies, utilities, and other major landowners 
and infrastructure owners, including, but not limited to, Canada Mortgage 
and Housing Corporation in respect of Gr anville Island, the Department of 
National Defence and/or the Canadian Coast Guard in respect of the 
Kitsilano Coast Guard Station, Fisheries and Oceans Canada in respect 
of the False Creek Harbour Authority, PAVCo in respect of B.C. Place, 
Providence Heal th Care in respect to St. Paul's Hospital, Vancouver 
Fraser Port Authority, Vancouver Airport Authority, TransLink, BC Hydro, 
Creative Energy, and such other affected public or regulated entities as 
staff may determine;  
 
4. identifying advocacy, research, technical, and partnership work required 
to support any future funding strategy or senior -government engagement; 
and 
 
5.         reporting back on options for a fiscally prudent convening and 
coordination role for the City that does not prejudice future Council 
decisions on capital commitments.  
 
C.  THAT the report back in B  above  also assesses whether similar 
multi -jurisdictional coordination challenges exist in the Burrard Inlet and Fraser 
River floodplains, or whether differences in land ownership, infrastructure 
ownership, and distribution of benefits mean that a False Creek -specific 
approach is warranted.  
 
LOST (Vote No. 11516)  
(Councillor s Dominato, Klassen, Meiszner, Montague, Zhou and Mayor Sim opposed)  
(Councillors Bligh and Kirby -Yung absent for the vote)  
 
  
 
Council Meeting  
Minutes, April 14, 2026  11 
 
 
The amendment having lost, the motion was put and CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 
11517) with Councillor s Bligh and Kirby -Yung absent for the vote .

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver Council urges senior governments to fund housing, shelters, and income supports
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council reviewed the 2025 Homeless Count results and formally asked the Provincial and Federal governments to act. The requests include higher income supports, a new navigation centre (a hub that connects people to services), expanded treatment programs, better discharge planning (helping people leaving hospitals or jails find stable housing), and more daytime shelter and supported housing options.
minutes_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Healthcare, Professional Services

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver Council approves $9.4M+ in cultural grants across four funding streams
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has approved more than $9.4 million in cultural grants covering institutional, annual, and three-year operating support, plus capital grants (one-time funding for physical projects or buildings) of $250,000 to the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society and $728,000 to Historic Joy Kogawa House Society.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Check Vancouver's cultural grants portal to see if your organization is eligible to apply for upcoming funding streams.

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver approves $89,000 in sport event grants for 19 community organizations, Spring 2026
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has approved 19 Community Sport Event Grants totalling $89,000 from the 2026 Vancouver Community Sport Hosting Program Budget. These grants support organizations hosting community sport events in Vancouver.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Check if your organization qualifies for future Vancouver Community Sport Event Grant rounds and apply early.

minutes_briefs.title: Property Tax Exemptions Report Reviewed by Council — No Changes Made
minutes_briefs.summary: Local council reviewed a report on 2025 property tax exemptions (reductions in the amount of property tax certain owners must pay) and accepted it for information only. No new decisions or rule changes were made as a result of this review.

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver adopts Coastal Flood Management Policy to guide flood risk planning and infrastructure
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has officially adopted a Coastal Flood Management Policy. This policy sets out the City's responsibilities for planning and building both physical barriers (like seawalls) and non-physical measures (like zoning rules) to protect against coastal flooding.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Check if your property or business is in a coastal flood zone and review how this policy may affect future permits or development plans.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Natural Resources

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver Heritage Foundation receives $152,375 grant under new 2026–2028 operating agreement
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved a $152,375 operating grant to the Vancouver Heritage Foundation for 2026, the first year of a new three-year funding agreement running from 2026 to 2028. Council also accepted the Foundation's 2025 Annual Report at the same meeting.

minutes_briefs.title: Public plaza alcohol consumption now permitted under amended City Land Regulation By-law through 2028
minutes_briefs.summary: City Council approved changes to City Land Regulation By-law No. 8735 that allow people to drink alcohol in specific designated public plazas. This rule is in place until 2028, opening new opportunities for businesses that serve or sell liquor near these public spaces.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Check if your business is near a designated plaza and review updated rules for serving or selling alcohol nearby.

key_dates.label: City Land Regulation By-law alcohol in public plazas — rule expires

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver applies for $200,000 to speed up housing development approvals
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has approved applying for $200,000 in provincial funding to redesign how the city processes housing development applications. If the funding is approved, the project aims to make it faster and easier to get housing projects approved, which could affect timelines for developers and property owners.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you develop or manage housing in Vancouver, watch for updates on approval process changes in 2026.

minutes_briefs.title: Housing rezoning conditions updated for multiple developments through new Housing Agreement By-laws
minutes_briefs.summary: City council approved changes to the rezoning conditions (the rules attached to a property's approved land-use change) for several housing developments. Council also directed staff to prepare Housing Agreement By-laws, which are legal contracts registered on a property's title that lock in specific housing requirements such as affordability or rental use.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own or are developing an affected property, review the updated rezoning conditions with your planner or lawyer.

minutes_briefs.title: 5590 Victoria Drive rezoned: affordable rental units converted to market-rate rental
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved a zoning change for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. The building's units, which were locked in as affordable rental housing, will now be allowed to rent at market rates (whatever landlords can charge based on demand). The floor space ratio (the total buildable area relative to the lot size) also increased slightly, from 3.49 to 3.5.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own or manage rental property nearby, review how this market-rate shift may affect your local rental pricing strategy.
minutes_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development, Tenant

minutes_briefs.title: 4088 Granville Street rezoned for 31 rental units — density more than doubled
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved a rezoning (a change to the rules about how land can be used) at 4088 Granville Street. The site will shift from its old zoning category to CD-1 (a custom zoning designation tailored to a specific project), allowing three three-storey residential rental buildings with 31 units and more than doubling the allowed density from FSR 0.5 to 1.2 (FSR, or Floor Space Ratio, measures how much total floor area can be built relative to the lot size).
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own or lease nearby property, review how increased density and new rental units may affect your business.

minutes_briefs.title: 82 rental units approved: West 10th Avenue site rezoned to six-storey mixed-use building
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has approved in principle a rezoning (a change to the rules about how land can be used and what can be built on it) for properties at 3803–3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. The sites will shift from low-density residential zoning to a new custom zoning category, allowing a six-storey mixed-use building with 82 secured market rental units.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own or lease property nearby, review the approved rezoning details with your real estate or legal advisor.

minutes_briefs.title: 310 East 14th Avenue rezoned for 20-storey rental building with 20% below-market units
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has approved in principle a rezoning of 310 East 14th Avenue to allow a 20-storey rental building. The new zoning increases the allowable density (how much building can fit on the lot) and height to 60 metres, with 20% of units required to be rented at below-market rates.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own or lease property near 310 East 14th Avenue, review how this new development may affect your business.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

minutes_briefs.title: West 13th Avenue rezoned to higher-density residential — development rules now changed
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has approved rezoning 1305–1325 West 13th Avenue from R3-3 to R5-4 residential district (meaning the land can now support taller, denser housing than before). A Housing Agreement By-law (a legal contract tied to the property that sets conditions on how housing must be used) was approved alongside the rezoning.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own or lease property near 1305–1325 West 13th Ave, review how increased density may affect your business.

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver developers face new fees and zoning rules under proposed Amenity Cost Charge By-law
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has sent two proposed changes out for public feedback: a new Amenity Cost Charge By-law (a fee developers would pay to fund community amenities like parks and community centres) and updates to density bonus rules (which let developers build taller or bigger in exchange for community benefits) under Bill 16. No rules have changed yet — this is the consultation stage.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you work in development or construction in Vancouver, watch for public consultation opportunities to submit your feedback.

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver property owners on East Hastings may face new parking project charges in 2025
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council passed By-law No. 14669, which allows the city to charge (assess) nearby property owners to help cover the 2025 operating costs of the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project. This means property owners in that area could see a special charge added to their property tax bill to fund shared parking infrastructure on East Hastings Street.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in the East Hastings assessment area and review any city notices about your 2025 charges.
minutes_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Transportation & Logistics

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver property owners near Trounce Alley and Blood Alley to be charged 2025 maintenance costs
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has passed a by-law (a local law) that will charge nearby property owners a share of the 2025 upkeep costs for Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square. This is a 'local improvement assessment' (a special fee billed to properties that directly benefit from a city-funded project in their area).
minutes_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in the Trounce Alley or Blood Alley area and review your upcoming property tax bill for the new charge.
minutes_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services

minutes_briefs.title: Housing Agreement locked in for 320–360 West 2nd Avenue under Vancouver By-law 14668
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council passed a by-law creating a Housing Agreement (a legal contract registered on a property's title that locks in rules about how the housing must be used) for the building at 320–360 West 2nd Avenue. This agreement sets binding conditions on that specific property — such as who can live there or how rents may be structured — that must be followed by current and future owners.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own, lease, or plan to buy/develop property at 320–360 West 2nd Avenue, review By-law 14668 for your obligations.

minutes_briefs.title: Housing Agreement amended for three Vancouver properties on Prior Street, Malkin Avenue, and Station Street
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council passed a by-law (By-law No. 14667) to update the Housing Agreement — a legal contract that sets rules about how housing at a property must be used — covering three addresses: 450–496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue, and 1002 Station Street. The amendment changes the existing terms tied to these properties, though the specific new conditions were not detailed in the public notice.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own or manage property at these three addresses, review By-law No. 14667 with your lawyer to understand the new terms.

minutes_briefs.title: 1710–1730 East Pender Street rezoned to CD-1 for mixed-use development
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has passed a by-law (No. 14665) that rezones 1710–1730 East Pender Street to CD-1 (a custom zoning district that sets specific rules for what can be built and how the land can be used on that exact site). This means the permitted uses and building rules for this property have officially changed.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own, lease, or plan to operate at 1710–1730 East Pender St, review the new CD-1 zoning rules with your lawyer or planner.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

minutes_briefs.title: Zoning rules updated for 1700–1750 West 75th Avenue in Vancouver
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has amended the zoning by-law (a local rule that controls how a specific property can be used or developed) for the property at 1700–1750 West 75th Avenue. This change updates the CD-1 (198) rules that govern what can be built or operated at that address.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own, lease, or plan to develop property at 1700–1750 West 75th Avenue, review the updated zoning rules with your planner or lawyer.

minutes_briefs.title: 888–896 Cambie Street 29-storey mixed-use rezoning heads to Public Hearing
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has sent a rezoning application for 888–896 Cambie Street and 111–191 Smithe Street to a Public Hearing. The proposal would allow a 29-storey building with 165 rental units and 246 hotel rooms, and would protect the facade of the heritage-designated Stanley Brock Building.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Watch for the Public Hearing date and attend or submit comments if this development affects your business.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt

minutes_briefs.title: East 12th Avenue site rezoned to CD-1, opening door to new development
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has passed a by-law rezoning the properties at 1302–1318 East 12th Avenue to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development), a special zoning category that sets custom rules for what can be built on a specific site. This means the land can now be developed under those new rules, which may affect nearby property owners, tenants, and businesses.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own, lease, or operate near 1302–1318 East 12th Avenue, review the CD-1 zoning rules to understand how future development may affect you.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

minutes_briefs.title: 6486 Chester Street rezoned to CD-1, opening door to new development
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has passed a by-law that rezones 6486 Chester Street to CD-1 (a custom zoning category that sets specific rules for what can be built on a single site). This means the land at that address can now be developed under new rules tailored to that specific property.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own, lease, or plan to develop property near 6486 Chester Street, review the new CD-1 zoning rules with your planner or lawyer.

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver creates C-2A zoning district to speed up rental housing and small hotel approvals
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has approved a new zoning category called C-2A (a mixed-use commercial district that allows both businesses and housing in the same building) to make it faster and easier to build rental apartments and small hotels. Several city bylaws — including rules on zoning, parking, signage, and noise — are being updated to support this new district.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Check if your property or development plans fall within a C-2A zone and review updated bylaw rules with your planner.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt

minutes_briefs.title: 16-storey hotel approved in principle at 888 West 8th Avenue, Vancouver
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has approved in principle a rezoning (a change to the rules about how land can be used) at 888 West 8th Avenue. The site will shift from its current zoning to CD-1 (a custom zoning category) to allow a 16-storey building with 152 hotel rooms and ground-floor commercial space.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you operate nearby or plan to bid on construction or supply contracts, monitor this project for final approval.

minutes_briefs.title: City approves $2.39B budget with $1.22B property tax levy for 2026
minutes_briefs.summary: City council has approved its 2026 budget, setting total spending at $2.39 billion and a General Purposes Tax Levy (the portion of city costs funded directly by property taxes) of $1.22 billion. This means property owners and businesses that pay property tax can expect their bills to reflect this levy when 2026 tax notices are issued.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review your 2026 property tax notice when it arrives and update your business budget to account for any increase.
minutes_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services

minutes_briefs.title: 2026 property tax split locks in 42.5% share for non-residential businesses
minutes_briefs.summary: Council has set the 2026 property tax distribution at 57.5% for residential properties and 42.5% for non-residential properties (meaning businesses, commercial, and industrial owners). The Director of Finance has been directed to calculate the actual tax rates that hit these targets.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review your 2026 property tax budget once new rates are published by the Director of Finance.

key_dates.label: 2026 non-residential property tax rates take effect

minutes_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development
